title: Miller Enterprises Ltd. and anor v. Jason Watson et. al.(Costs)
text:
COMMONWEALTH OF THE BAHAMAS
IN THE COURT OF APPEAL
SCCivApp. No. 161 of 2025
B E T W E E N
MILLER ENTERPRISES LIMITED
First Appellant
EARL MILLER
Second Appellant
AND
JASON WATSON, HAROLD L. WATSON AND GERARD MORTIMER
In their capacity as TRUSTEES OF The Automotive & Industrial Distributors Limited
Staff Retirement Fund
Respondents
BEFORE: The Honourable Mr. Justice Turner, JA
APPEARANCES: Mr. D. Halson Moultrie, with Mrs. Rose Green-Thompson, and Mr.
Moses Bain, Counsel for the Appellants
Ms. Michelle Deveaux, with Ms. Adrienne Bellot, Counsel for the
Respondents
DATES: 13 April 2026
************************************
Civil Appeal – Certificate of Urgency – Stay of Application – Service of the Notice of Appeal –
Abortive Hearing – Costs – Rules 71.3 and 71.6(1) and (3) of the Supreme Court Civil Procedure
Rules – Section 30(1) of the Supreme Court Act – Rule 24 of the Court of Appeal Rules
The Appellants filed a Notice of Appeal on 18 September 2025, and subsequently a Certificate of
Urgency on 10 October 2025, seeking to challenge an order of the Supreme Court requiring them
to deliver up possession of property by 20 October 2025 and restraining their continued
occupation. The Certificate of Urgency represented that, absent urgent intervention, the Appellants
would suffer severe prejudice, including eviction, loss of business operations, and the rendering of
the appeal nugatory. The matter was accordingly listed for urgent hearing before this Court on 15
October 2025.
Upon the matter being called for hearing, it became apparent that the appeal had not been properly
constituted. The Notice of Appeal had not been timely served, no application for a stay had been

determined in the court below, and no interlocutory application had been brought before the Court.
The Appellants conceded that they were not in a position to proceed. The hearing did not take
place, and the Respondents, having attended prepared to argue the matter, sought their costs.
The Court reserved its decision on costs.
Held: The Respondents’ application for costs is allowed up to and including 15 October 2025, to
be taxed if not agreed.
The Court found that the Appellants had, by their Certificate of Urgency, represented to the Court
that the matter required immediate intervention, yet failed to ensure that any application was
properly constituted.
The consequence of that failure fell directly upon the Respondents, who, in response to the urgent
listing, prepared submissions and attended ready to proceed. The costs thereby incurred were
plainly occasioned by the Appellants’ default.
Accordingly, the Court was satisfied that the Respondents were entitled to their costs up to and
including 15 October 2025.
Bernard Schulte Shipmanagement (Cyprus) Ltd. v. Ritchie and another [2015] 3 BHS J. No. 152,
applied
Swart et al v. Appollon Metaxides, Silver Point Condominium Apt SCCivApp No.78 of 2012,
applied
_____________________________________________________________________________
DECISION ON COSTS
_____________________________________________________________________________
Judgment delivered by The Honourable Mr. Justice Turner, JA:
Introduction
1. This decision on costs arises from a hearing before a single judge of this Court on 15 October
2025. Although listed for hearing on an urgent basis, no substantive application was capable
of proceeding. The sole issue which now falls for determination is the appropriate order as to
costs in circumstances where the Appellants failed to properly place any appeal or application
before the Court.
2. The Appellants initiated the appellate process by filing a Notice of Appeal on 18 September
2025. This was followed by a Certificate of Urgency dated 10 October 2025, by which the
Appellants sought to invoke the Court’s urgent jurisdiction.
3. The Certificate of Urgency provides as follows:
“1) By Order in Supreme Court Action No.
2025/CLE/gen/00380 Madam Justice Carla Card-Stubbs issued
an Order:
2

a. That the Appellants do forthwith deliver up possession
of the said property before 20th October, 2025; and
b. Restraining the Appellants their servants, employees
and/or agents from further occupying the land;
2) The nature and effect of the relief granted by the Learned
Judge has a substantive effect on the final relief sought by the
Respondents thus makin (sic) it unnessary (sic) for a trial.
3) The purported purchase and sale of the Appellants’ property
identified by Assessment No. 72874, is in violation of multiple
sections of the Real Property Tax Act, namely, sections 25A and
25B as Amended in 2022 and 2024 or any other amendment.
4) In the circumstances if this Notice of Appeal seeking to have
the interim injunction and order set aside and/or further stayed
is not immediately dispensed with the Appellants, in compliance
with the said order would be severely prejudiced, the Appellants
would be ruined, forced to vacate the property, rendered
homeless and forced to discontinue two businesses thereby
terminating the employment of twenty three (23) workers;
5) Further, if the 20th October, 2025 occurs before the appeal is
heard the rules of natural justice would stand breached,
significant damages are likely to occur to the Appellants’
property, the Appellants’ liberty would be exposed to the Penal
Notice attached to the Court’s Order and the appeal would be
rendered nugatory.”
4. It was on the strength of those representations that the matter was listed for urgent hearing
before a single judge of this Court.
5. However, upon the matter being called, it became apparent that the procedural foundation
necessary to sustain any hearing was wholly absent. The Notice of Appeal had not been served
within the time prescribed by the Court of Appeal Rules (“the CoA Rules”), service having
only been purportedly effected on the morning of the hearing. No application for a stay had
been determined in the court below, nor had any interlocutory application been properly
brought before this Court.
6. In those circumstances, counsel for the Appellants submitted that he had understood the matter
was listed for the settling of the record only, and not for a substantive hearing, and conceded
that he was not in a position to proceed. The hearing did not take place, and the Respondents,
having attended prepared to argue the matter, sought their costs.
7. The Court reserved its decision on costs.
3

Relevant Law
8. The Court’s jurisdiction to award costs in appellate proceedings is derived from both statute
and the applicable rules of court. At the appellate level, that jurisdiction is expressly reflected
in Rule 24 of the CoA Rules, which provides, so far as material:
“24. (1) In relation to an appeal, the court shall have all the
powers and duties as to amendment and otherwise of the
Supreme Court.
…
(5) The court may make such order as to the whole or any part
of the costs of an appeal as may be just, and may, in special
circumstances, order that such security shall be given for the
costs of an appeal as may be just.”
9. This provision is to be read alongside, and derives substance from, the broader statutory
discretion conferred by section 30(1) of the Supreme Court Act, which provides:
“30. (1) Subject to this or any other Act and to rules of court, the
costs of and incidental to all proceedings in the Court, including
the administration of estates and trusts, shall be in the discretion
of the Court or judge and the Court or judge shall have full
power to determine by whom and to what extent the costs are to
be paid.”
10. In practical terms, and consistent with established appellate practice, the exercise of that
discretion is informed by the principles applicable in the Supreme Court. Those principles are
now encapsulated in the Supreme Court Civil Procedure Rules, 2022 (“the CPR”), in particular
Rules 71.3 and 71.6, which provide:
“71.3 The court’s powers to make orders about costs include
power to make orders requiring a party to pay the costs of
another person arising out of or related to all or any part of any
proceedings.
…
71.6 (1) Where the court decides to make an order about the
costs of any proceedings, the general rule is that it must order
the unsuccessful party to pay the costs of the successful party.
…
(3) Without limiting the court’s discretion or the range of orders
open to it, the court may order a person to pay —
(a) costs from or up to a certain date only;
4

(b) costs relating only to a certain distinct part of the
proceedings; or
(c) only a specified proportion of another person’s costs.”
11. These provisions make clear that, although the Court’s discretion as to costs is wide, it is not
unstructured, but must be exercised judicially, in accordance with settled principle.
12. That approach has been consistently affirmed by the authorities. In Swart et al v. Appollon
Metaxides, Silver Point Condominium Apt SCCivApp No.78 of 2012, Isaacs JA (as he then
was) stated:
“7. In the Supreme Court the issue of who should bear the costs
of an action and/or application falls to be considered in light of
Order 59 of the Rules of the Supreme Court [now rule 71 of the
Supreme Court Civil Procedure Rules, 2022]. Moreover, section
30(1) of the Supreme Court Act provides:
‘30. (1) … the costs of and incidental to all proceedings …
shall be in the discretion of the Court or judge…’
8. We generally have regard to the practice that obtains in the
Supreme Court. In my view this makes estimably good sense.’”
13. Accordingly, this Court, in the exercise of its own jurisdiction, will ordinarily apply the same
underlying principles.
14. Foremost among those principles is the well-established general rule that costs follow the
event. In Bernard Schulte Shipmanagement (Cyprus) Ltd. v. Ritchie and another [2015]
3 BHS J. No. 152, the court affirmed that a successful party is ordinarily entitled to its costs
unless there are circumstances which warrant a departure from the usual rule.
15. It is also material to observe that the jurisdiction to award costs is not contingent upon the
determination of a substantive appeal. Rule 71.6(3)(a) of the CPR expressly contemplates that
costs may be awarded “up to a certain date only”, thereby permitting an award of costs in
respect of an abortive hearing.
Discussion
16. Applying these principles, the Appellants, by filing a Notice of Appeal and a Certificate of
Urgency, invoked the jurisdiction of this Court and procured the listing of an urgent hearing.
17. That step carried with it a corresponding obligation to ensure procedural readiness. Instead, the
matter came before the Court in a state where no application was properly constituted. The
defects were not merely technical but fundamental: there had been no timely service of the
Notice of Appeal, no stay application determined in the court below, and no interlocutory
application before this Court.
5

18. In those circumstances, the matter was incapable of proceeding. This was not a case in which
the Appellants failed on the merits; rather, it was one in which they failed to place any
justiciable issue before the Court at all.
19. The position is rendered more acute by the fact that the Appellants had, by their Certificate of
Urgency, represented to this Court that the matter required immediate intervention, yet failed
to ensure that any application was properly constituted.
20. The consequence of that failure fell directly upon the Respondents, who, in response to the
urgent listing, prepared submissions and attended ready to proceed. The costs thereby incurred
were plainly occasioned by the Appellants’ default.
21. In those circumstances, the application of the general rule admits little difficulty. The
Appellants, having set the process in motion and failed to prosecute it in accordance with the
CoA Rules, must bear the costs thereby occasioned.
22. Further, this is a proper case for an order limited to the costs of the abortive hearing.
Disposition
23. For the foregoing reasons, the Court is satisfied that the Respondents are entitled to their costs
up to and including 15 October 2025, such costs to be taxed if not agreed.
_____________________________________
The Honourable Mr. Justice Turner, JA
6
judges:
ments - Court of Appeal of the Commonwealth of The Bahamas
